description: ClawHQ backlog — aligned with evolved strategy (2026-03-28). See docs/STRATEGY.md for strategic context.

items.title: Sentinel monitoring service — upstream intelligence that a cron job can't replicate
items.intent: ClawHQ must have a day-one revenue hypothesis. Sentinel is a paid monitoring service that provides upstream intelligence — pre-computing config breakage against incoming OpenClaw commits, tracking CVE impact per blueprint, and alerting on skill dependency changes. This must do something a local cron job cannot.
items.description: The evolved strategy requires testing revenue from day one, not deferring it. Sentinel is the hypothesis: ~$19/month monitoring that enhances sovereignty rather than compromising it. CRITICAL DESIGN CONSTRAINT: this must NOT be 'clawhq doctor --watch' as a service — that's replicable with a cron job and the sovereignty audience will call it out immediately. The value is upstream intelligence: (1) monitor OpenClaw GitHub commits and pre-compute whether your specific config will break before you update, (2) track new CVEs and map them to your installed skills/tools, (3) detect when upstream dependencies of your skills change, (4) aggregate anonymized health signals across the fleet to identify emerging issues before they hit you. Phase 1 (MVP): OpenClaw commit monitoring + config breakage prediction. Phase 2: CVE mapping + skill dependency tracking.
steps: Design Sentinel architecture: upstream monitor (GitHub API polling), config analyzer (diff against user's blueprint), alert delivery (email/webhook)
Implement OpenClaw commit monitor: poll GitHub API for new commits to main, parse changed files, identify config-impacting changes
Implement config breakage predictor: given a commit diff and a user's compiled config, determine if the update would break their deployment
Implement alert system: email or webhook notification with specific breakage details and recommended action
Design pricing page / signup flow for revenue validation
Build minimal web endpoint for Sentinel subscription management
Add clawhq cloud sentinel connect command to link a deployment to Sentinel
Phase 2: CVE tracker that maps advisories to user's specific skill/tool inventory
acceptanceCriteria: Sentinel monitors OpenClaw upstream commits and identifies config-impacting changes
Users receive alerts BEFORE updating when a new release would break their specific config
Alert includes: what changed upstream, what would break in their config, recommended action
Service provides value that a local cron job cannot replicate (upstream intelligence, not local health checks)
Pricing page exists and accepts signups for revenue validation
Revenue signal measurable within 30 days of announcement
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Day-zero revenue experiment. Not sprint-ready — needs architecture design. Key insight from critique: must do something a cron job can't. WebSocket health checks are trivially replicable. Upstream commit analysis + config breakage prediction is genuinely valuable and requires centralized infrastructure. Phase 1 can be technically simple (GitHub API + config diff) but the product design needs work first.

items.title: Development-as-content pipeline — every commit generates discoverable content
items.intent: ClawHQ's development work must produce discoverable content as a byproduct. One-time launch events decay in 72 hours. Compounding content solves the discovery problem without a separate content calendar.
items.description: The evolved strategy identified discovery decay as a critical wound. The fix: every development action produces content. Bug fix → postmortem article. New blueprint → tutorial walkthrough. OpenClaw breaking change → 'what broke and how we fixed it.' This is a process change, not a feature. Implementation: (1) CHANGELOG entries written as narratives, not version lists, (2) template for converting commits/PRs into technical articles, (3) docs/articles/ directory for long-form content derived from development, (4) PROBLEMS.md already exists and is evergreen SEO — formalize the update process.
steps: Create docs/articles/ directory for technical content
Create article template: title, problem, context, solution, what we learned, ClawHQ relevance
Rewrite CHANGELOG.md entries as narratives (not just version bumps)
Write first article from existing work: '14 Ways Your OpenClaw Agent Silently Breaks' (from PROBLEMS.md content)
Write second article from BUG-125: 'Why Regex YAML Patching Will Corrupt Your Docker Config'
Add CONTRIBUTING.md section on development-as-content expectations
Document the process: each PR that fixes a landmine or adds a blueprint should include a content draft
acceptanceCriteria: docs/articles/ directory exists with article template
At least 2 articles written from existing development work
CHANGELOG.md entries are narrative, not version lists
Process documented in CONTRIBUTING.md for future development
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Zero-cost discovery fix. The content already exists in the codebase — PROBLEMS.md, OPENCLAW-REFERENCE.md, bug fixes. This is about packaging it. Sprint-ready — content work, not code. The '14 ways' article could be the HN launch piece.

items.title: Assumption validation framework — concrete tests for the 3 riskiest strategic assumptions
items.intent: Strategy without validation is fiction. The 3 highest-risk assumptions must have concrete pass/fail criteria, measurement methods, and deadlines so the team knows when to pivot.
items.description: The evolved strategy identified 7 assumptions, 3 critical. Each needs a concrete test: (1) Addressable demand — measure clone→install conversion in first 1,000 visitors (pass: >5%), (2) Willingness to pay — Sentinel landing page signups (pass: 10+ in 60 days), (3) Foundation absorption — monitor OpenClaw PRs for guided config (signal: 2+ PRs in 6 months). Implementation: tracking dashboard or simple JSON file that records metrics against targets, reviewed monthly.
steps: Create backlog/assumptions.json with the 3 testable assumptions, pass/fail criteria, and deadlines
Add GitHub repo analytics tracking plan (stars, clones, installs via curl script)
Design Sentinel landing page for willingness-to-pay test
Set up OpenClaw GitHub watch for configuration-related PRs/RFCs
Document review cadence: monthly assumption review, update assumptions.json with latest data
acceptanceCriteria: backlog/assumptions.json documents all 3 assumptions with pass/fail criteria and deadlines
Each assumption has a concrete measurement method that doesn't require guessing
Monthly review cadence is documented
First review date set for 30 days after repo goes public
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Converts 'direction locked' into something testable. Without this, the strategy is faith, not science. Sprint-ready — primarily documentation work. Can be done in a single sprint.

items.title: Reduce process.exit() usage in CLI — return exit codes or throw for testability
items.intent: Make CLI command handlers testable and allow proper cleanup. Commands should throw or return exit codes instead of calling process.exit() directly.
items.description: Architecture review found 192 direct process.exit() calls. This prevents testing error paths, blocks defer/cleanup handlers, and makes graceful shutdown impossible.
steps: Create a CommandError class that carries an exit code
Replace process.exit() calls with throw CommandError in command handlers
Add try/finally blocks around spinner usage to ensure cleanup
Wire top-level parseAsync catch to extract exit codes from CommandError
Add unit tests for error paths in key commands (deploy, destroy, scan)
acceptanceCriteria: Command handlers throw errors or return exit codes instead of calling process.exit()
A single top-level error handler in the CLI entry point calls process.exit()
Spinners are properly stopped in error paths via try/finally
At least 3 command handlers have unit tests covering error paths
items.groomingNotes: Blocked by FEAT-108. After decomposition, each command file can be fixed independently. process.exit() cleanup is per-command surgery — doing it in a 4320-line index.ts carries high regression risk.

items.title: 1Password integration — agent-accessible credential vault via service account tokens
items.intent: Agents need credential access for email, logins, API calls. Hardcoded env vars have no rotation or audit. 1Password service accounts give scoped read-only access with full audit trail — aligned with data sovereignty and security-by-default.
items.description: Integrate 1Password service account model into agent runtime. Architecture: user's 1Password account has an Agent Vault with only the credentials the agent needs. Agent gets a service account token (OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN) injected as Docker secret. op CLI or Python SDK installed in container. Helper utility claw-secret wraps credential fetch with logging. Security model: read-only, fetch-at-use (never persisted), never in LLM context, scoped vault, full audit, instant revoke, quarterly rotation.
steps: Add op CLI or 1Password Python SDK to the Docker build stage (src/build/docker/)
Add OP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN to credential store (src/secure/credentials/) with Docker secret injection
Create claw-secret helper utility that wraps op read with logging and never exposes credentials to stdout/LLM context
Add credential probe for 1Password service account token validity (src/secure/credentials/probes.ts)
Add blueprint configuration for 1Password vault name and credential mapping
Add doctor check: 1Password service account token valid, vault accessible, op CLI installed
Add tests for credential fetch, token validation, error handling
acceptanceCriteria: op CLI or Python SDK available in agent container after clawhq build
Service account token injected via Docker secrets, never in env vars or config files
claw-secret utility fetches credentials with audit logging
Credentials never appear in LLM context, agent memory files, or logs
clawhq doctor checks 1Password connectivity and token validity
Blueprint supports 1Password vault configuration
items.groomingNotes: From GitHub issue #8. Core credential access pattern needed for any agent that interacts with external services. Aligns with security-by-default and data sovereignty.

items.title: Model routing per cron job — haiku for cheap scans, opus for deep work, with fallback chains
items.intent: Cron jobs must be cost-efficient. A heartbeat check that runs every 10 minutes should not use the most expensive model. Each job must specify its model with a fallback chain.
items.description: Clawdius routes cron jobs to different models: heartbeat and x-scan use haiku (cheap, fast) with sonnet fallback, while work-session uses opus (expensive, capable) with sonnet fallback. ClawHQ's blueprint cron_config only specifies schedule expressions — no model routing.
steps: Extend blueprint cron_config schema to support model and fallbacks per job
Update config generator (src/design/configure/generate.ts) to emit model and fallbacks in cron job payloads
Update all 7 blueprints with sensible model routing (heartbeat→haiku, work_session→opus, morning_brief→sonnet)
Add wizard question or smart inference for model routing preferences
Add validation: specified models must be available in the configured provider
Add tests for cron job generation with model routing
acceptanceCriteria: Blueprint cron_config supports model and fallbacks per job type
Generated cron jobs include payload.model and payload.fallbacks fields
Default model routing follows cost-efficiency: cheap models for frequent jobs, capable models for deep work
All 7 blueprints have sensible model routing defaults
Validation catches references to unavailable models
items.groomingNotes: Direct cost impact for every user. Without model routing, an agent running 10-minute heartbeats on opus burns through budget unnecessarily.

items.title: Integration tests for cloud provisioning engine — test real registry, health polling, and state transitions
items.intent: The provisioning engine (engine.ts) is tested with every dependency mocked — credentials, provider adapter, health poller, and registry are all fakes. State transitions (provisioning → active → destroyed) have never been tested against real state persistence.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/engine.test.ts mocks 4 modules. The test verifies that the engine calls the right mocks in the right order — it does not verify that the engine produces correct state.
steps: Create integration test: engine.integration.test.ts
Use real filesystem (mkdtempSync) for registry state (instances.json)
Use real health polling against a local HTTP test server
Mock only the provider adapter (can't create real VMs in CI)
Test full state machine: provision → health check → register → status query → destroy → verify cleanup
Test error paths: provision succeeds but health fails, destroy fails, registry write fails
Run npm test to confirm no regression
acceptanceCriteria: Integration test exercises the full provisioning state machine with real filesystem and real HTTP
Only the cloud provider API is mocked — everything else is real
Registry state is verified by reading the actual instances.json file
Health polling is tested against a real local HTTP server
All existing engine unit tests continue to pass
items.groomingNotes: Most heavily mocked test file in the codebase. Real state machine testing deferred until after Gate 0 blockers.

items.title: Extended identity files — USER.md, TOOLS.md, and domain-specific runbooks in blueprint config
items.intent: An agent that only knows its personality (SOUL.md) and capabilities (AGENTS.md) is incomplete. It must also understand its user's context, have a role-organized tool reference, and carry domain-specific operating procedures.
items.description: Clawdius has 7 identity/reference files beyond SOUL.md and AGENTS.md. ClawHQ only generates SOUL.md + AGENTS.md. The delta is significant: without USER.md, the agent doesn't know who it's serving.
steps: Extend wizard to collect user context: name, timezone, communication preferences, key constraints
Add USER.md generator to src/design/identity/
Add TOOLS.md generator — organizes blueprint tools by role/use-case with descriptions
Add blueprint-specific runbook support: each blueprint can define additional identity files
Update identity file generation in generate.ts to include new files
Update Docker build to copy all identity files, not just SOUL.md and AGENTS.md
Add tests for USER.md and TOOLS.md generation from various blueprints
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq init collects user context (name, timezone, preferences) via wizard
Generated deployment includes USER.md with user profile
Generated deployment includes TOOLS.md with role-organized tool reference
Blueprints can define additional identity files (runbooks) in their YAML
All identity files are mounted read-only in the container
Token budget validation accounts for all identity files
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Supports '10 curated blueprints' goal — each masterclass blueprint needs rich identity files. Not sprint-ready — needs design for blueprint-specific runbooks.

items.title: Construct meta-skill — autonomous self-improvement loop for agent capability growth
items.intent: Agents must grow their capabilities over time without manual intervention. The construct skill is the mechanism: assess gaps → propose new skills → build → deploy → validate. This is THE differentiator for the 'agent grows' pillar.
items.description: Clawdius has a 'construct' skill that runs daily at 2am PT. Over 3 months it has autonomously built 7 skills. ClawHQ's skill system supports install/remove/update but has no autonomous growth mechanism.
steps: Design construct skill config for ClawHQ: SKILL.md, state machine (assess/propose/build/deploy/validate), cron schedule
Add construct to configs/skills/ as a built-in skill
Implement state persistence (construct tracks what it has assessed, proposed, built)
Wire construct into blueprint cron_config as an optional daily job
Add skill vetting integration — construct-built skills must pass the same vetting as manually installed skills
Add construct to 'replace-my-pa', 'founders-ops', and 'research-copilot' blueprints by default
Add tests for state machine transitions and skill output validation
acceptanceCriteria: Construct skill is available as a built-in skill in configs/skills/
Construct follows Assess→Propose→Build→Deploy→Validate cycle
Construct-built skills pass the same vetting pipeline as manually installed skills
State is persisted across runs (no redundant assessments)
Blueprints can opt-in to construct via skill_bundle configuration
At least 3 blueprints include construct by default
items.groomingNotes: Extracted from Clawdius. 'Agent grows' pillar differentiator. Not sprint-ready — needs design for vetting pipeline integration.

items.title: Operational automation scripts — auto-update, security monitoring, workspace backup generation
items.intent: A deployed agent must maintain itself. Auto-updates, security advisory monitoring, and workspace backups should be generated by ClawHQ, not hand-written by the operator.
items.description: Clawdius has 24 operational scripts. ClawHQ has doctor and backup commands but does not generate the operational automation layer.
steps: Generate auto-update script during clawhq init
Generate security monitor script (checks GitHub Security Advisories for OpenClaw CVEs)
Generate workspace backup script (incremental rsync to configurable backup dir)
Generate systemd unit files for firewall watcher and backup timer
Add clawhq ops install command to deploy generated scripts and timers
Add doctor checks: auto-update timer active, backup recent, security monitor running
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq init generates operational scripts in the deployment directory
clawhq ops install deploys scripts as systemd timers/services
Auto-update runs on configurable schedule (default: daily)
Security monitor checks GitHub Advisories and alerts on critical/high CVEs
Workspace backup runs on configurable schedule with retention policy
clawhq doctor reports operational automation status
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Supports 'agent grows' pillar. Auto-update and security monitoring tie into Sentinel value prop. Not sprint-ready — needs design for multi-instance interaction.

items.title: Domain-based egress firewall with ipset — upgrade from IP-based iptables to DNS-resolved allowlists
items.intent: Agent egress must be restricted to explicitly allowed domains, not IP addresses. IP-based rules miss CDN rotations, multi-homed services, and IPv6.
items.description: ClawHQ's firewall module uses IP-based iptables rules. Clawdius uses domain-based egress: a domain allowlist resolved via DNS into an ipset, refreshed every 5 minutes.
steps: Add domain-allowlist concept to blueprint security_posture
Generate domain allowlist file from blueprint egress config during clawhq init
Implement ipset creation from domain allowlist
Update firewall module to use ipset match rules instead of per-IP iptables rules
Generate refresh mechanism for periodic DNS re-resolution
Add doctor check: ipset exists, not stale, matches allowlist
Add tests for domain resolution, ipset creation, stale detection
acceptanceCriteria: Blueprint security posture includes a domain allowlist
clawhq up creates an ipset from DNS resolution of the allowlist
iptables rules reference the ipset, not individual IP addresses
A refresh mechanism keeps the ipset current
clawhq doctor detects stale ipsets
clawhq down removes the ipset and iptables rules cleanly
items.groomingNotes: Extracted from Clawdius. Not sprint-ready — needs design for ipset lifecycle with multi-instance.

items.title: Integration tests for credential probes — test against real API endpoints with test keys
items.intent: Credential health probes are tested with mocked fetch. If an API changes its auth error response, our probe will misinterpret it. Real probes against real APIs must be tested.
items.description: Live probe tests that run when test API keys are available. Ollama probe can always run locally.
steps: Add integration test: src/secure/credentials/probes.integration.test.ts
For each probe: if env var with test key is set, run real probe
Ollama probe can always run if Ollama is installed locally
Validate: probe returns valid:true for good keys, valid:false for bad keys
Skip gracefully when no test keys are available
acceptanceCriteria: Live probe tests run against real APIs when test credentials are available
Ollama probe runs against local Ollama when available
Tests skip gracefully when no credentials are provided
Existing mock-based unit tests remain unchanged
items.groomingNotes: Lower priority — probe API calls are simple HTTP and unlikely to break. Quick win for Ollama probe.

items.title: Stoic coach personality blueprint — purpose-built agent for philosophical mentorship
items.intent: The stoic coach blueprint proves ClawHQ can forge agents with deep personality — not just task automation, but genuine character. This is the blueprint that makes people say 'this is different from ChatGPT'.
items.description: Extract Clawdius's stoic identity patterns into a 'Stoic Coach' blueprint. Part of the '10 curated blueprints' strategy — this is blueprint #8.
steps: Create configs/blueprints/stoic-coach.yaml
Define personality: high analyticalDepth, moderate warmth, high directness, low verbosity
Define customization questions: philosophical tradition, practice times, accountability style
Define toolbelt: journal (append-only markdown), reflection-prompt generator
Define skills: daily-stoic (morning prompt + evening reflection), weekly-review
Define cron, autonomy, and memory policy
Test: clawhq init --guided with stoic-coach produces a deployable agent config
acceptanceCriteria: stoic-coach.yaml is a complete blueprint with all required sections
Personality dimensions are distinct from other blueprints
Blueprint includes at least one unique tool and one unique skill
clawhq init --guided with stoic-coach produces a deployable agent config
The forged agent has a distinctive voice — tested by reading generated SOUL.md
items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY: Part of '10 curated blueprints' goal. Blueprint #8 (current 7 + stoic coach). Not sprint-ready — needs journal tool generator and daily-stoic skill.

items.title: JMAP email backend — FastMail-compatible email tool as alternative to himalaya/IMAP
items.intent: Users on FastMail (or any JMAP provider) must have a native email tool that uses JMAP instead of IMAP.
items.description: Clawdius has a battle-tested email-fastmail.py (stdlib Python, no deps) implementing JMAP. ClawHQ's email tool generator only produces himalaya-based IMAP scripts.
steps: Add JMAP email backend to src/design/tools/email.ts
Generate email-jmap.py (stdlib Python, no deps)
Add JMAP credential collection to wizard
Add account switching support (--account flag)
Add doctor check: JMAP session endpoint reachable, token valid
Add to email-manager and replace-my-pa blueprints
acceptanceCriteria: Email tool generator can produce JMAP-based scripts as alternative to himalaya
Generated JMAP tool uses stdlib Python only
Account switching works between JMAP and himalaya backends
clawhq doctor checks JMAP connectivity when configured
items.groomingNotes: Extracted from Clawdius. JMAP is superior to IMAP for agent use cases. Not sprint-ready — needs design for JMAP/himalaya coexistence.

items.title: News aggregator tool — multi-source scan with deduplication and interest-graph filtering
items.intent: Agents that monitor information sources need a unified news aggregator. Foundation for any monitoring or research blueprint.
items.description: Clawdius has a 'news' tool that scans X + Substack + Tavily by interest topic, deduplicates, and manages API budget.
steps: Design news tool API: scan by interest, deduplicate, budget management
Design interests tool API: add/remove topics, set priorities, manage sources
Add tool generators to src/design/tools/
Generate interests.json with default interests from blueprint
Add to research-copilot, founders-ops blueprints
Add tests for deduplication logic and budget management
acceptanceCriteria: News tool scans and returns deduplicated, filtered results
Interests tool manages a persistent interest graph
Generated tools use existing tool dependencies as backends
Budget management prevents runaway API costs
items.groomingNotes: Extracted from Clawdius. Depends on Tavily and X tools working first. Lower priority than security and multi-instance.

items.title: Expand blueprint library to 10 reference masterclasses
items.intent: ClawHQ must ship with 10 curated reference blueprints, each a deep demonstration of a real use case. 7 blueprints exist. The remaining 3 must be production-quality — reading them should make a target user think 'this person understands how I actually work.'
items.description: Strategy fix #2 (Community) requires 10 curated reference blueprints, not a community marketplace. Current count: 7 blueprints. The gap is 3 more. Each blueprint must be a masterclass for its use case — selection criteria: (1) significant OpenClaw user population has this need, (2) the use case has well-known failure modes ClawHQ handles correctly by default, (3) demonstrates security-by-default in a concrete domain. Candidates: Research Copilot (Tavily, summarization, citation), Founders Ops (communications, scheduling, prioritization), Personal Finance Assistant (read-only bank feeds, alerts, summaries — no write access). Each requires identity file content, tool selection, skill bundle, cron jobs, security posture, and integration config.

acceptanceCriteria.text: Blueprint library contains exactly 10 blueprints, each with complete YAML (identity, tools, skills, cron, security_posture, autonomy)

acceptanceCriteria: Each new blueprint includes at least one domain-specific security constraint not in the default posture
All 10 blueprints validate against the blueprint spec (once FEAT-121 is complete)

acceptanceCriteria.text: Each blueprint has a corresponding tutorial draft in docs/articles/ for the dev-as-content pipeline

items.groomingNotes: STRATEGY fix #2 (Community). Gap identified during PO grooming 2026-03-28: strategy says '10 reference blueprints' but backlog had no item for the missing 3. Each blueprint is a content asset (tutorial draft in docs/articles/) and a quality signal for the Blueprint Specification (FEAT-121). Priority should because it follows Gate 0 — not blocking launch but must ship with repo. Candidates: Research Copilot, Founders Ops, Personal Finance Assistant. Depends on FEAT-121 for final validation. Not sprint-ready until FEAT-121 spec is done.

items.title: Deploy preflight should not hard-fail when Ollama is absent for local-model blueprints
items.intent: The e2e smoke test (FEAT-018) revealed that `clawhq up` hard-fails on preflight when Ollama is not installed, even though the Docker build succeeds. In CI environments and on machines without Ollama, the full user journey cannot complete. The preflight Ollama check should warn instead of block, or `--skip-preflight` should be documented as the CI workaround.
items.description: Discovered during FEAT-018 initial e2e run: `clawhq up -d <dir> --token <tok> --skip-firewall` exits 1 with 'Ollama is not installed — required for local model inference'. The blueprint uses local model provider, so preflight rejects deployment. This blocks the full user journey in any environment without Ollama. Options: (a) downgrade Ollama check to a warning when model routing is local, (b) allow --skip-preflight to bypass gracefully, (c) add a --no-ollama flag that switches to a no-model-needed mode for testing.
steps: Review preflight Ollama check in src/build/launcher/ — determine if it should be a hard gate or warning
For local-model blueprints, downgrade Ollama absence to a warning (agent can still start, just no inference)
Ensure --skip-preflight cleanly bypasses all preflight checks including Ollama
Update e2e smoke test to use --skip-preflight for the up step so CI can pass end-to-end
Add test coverage for preflight with and without Ollama
acceptanceCriteria: clawhq up completes (containers start) even when Ollama is not installed, with a clear warning
e2e smoke test passes the 'up' step in CI environments without Ollama
--skip-preflight flag cleanly bypasses the Ollama check
items.groomingNotes: Filed from FEAT-018 e2e smoke test initial run. The Ollama preflight hard-fail is the only integration gap found — all other steps (init, build, doctor, down, destroy) pass. This is a usability issue for CI and for users who want to deploy with cloud-only models.
